title: Evangelion and Marxism
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion and Marxism             Quote Post by Tumbling Down » Tue Oct 18, 2016 7:20 pm This excerpt from some interview was posted in the Shin Godzilla thread. Anno: When I really think it through I think that’s how it is. I don’t hate Star Trek, but I’m not that into it. You can see something of the arrogance of America [in it]. There is a story of influencing or enlightening the native people of the destination planets, or there is a romance with their most admirable woman in a front-line base. I feel like this is American imperialism itself.54 Komatsu: More than imperialism, it’s the imposition of a Christian sense of justice. Anno: Somehow this way Marxists are portrayed as being primitive people. I can’t get used to that kind of American worldview.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Marxism             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Oct 18, 2016 7:52 pm While it's true the NGE is very much about the individual, I'd argue it's as much about community as well, and behaving responsibly around others. Look at episode 2 when Misato shows Shinji the city he saved. Rebuild I think does this moreso: in 1.0, Shinji learns that he isn't alone and that he has an entire country of people supporting him and relying on him to make the right choice. In 2.0, he makes the wrong choice and selfishly casts off the world in favor of one person. In 3.0, he's no longer part of the community, and in an attempt to win back his place he acts even more irresponsibly. 4.0, I imagine, while feature him learning balance with others and finding a place where he can do good for himself as well as the community. Of course, this doesn't necessarily mean Marxism, but there is definitely a positive view of collectivism portrayed in Eva, as well as individualism.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Marxism             Quote Post by Tumbling Down » Tue Oct 18, 2016 8:03 pm Sachi wrote: In 2.0, he makes the wrong choice and selfishly casts off the world in favor of one person.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Marxism             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Oct 18, 2016 9:28 pm Tumbling Down wrote: He didn't know he was going to trigger the third impact. I mean, I'm not misremembering, right? I've seen this movie three times.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Marxism             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Tue Oct 18, 2016 9:48 pm As I said in that thread: SoryuUberAlles wrote: The Japan Communist Party is the largest 3I-party in the world and Marxist educators are still influential in a lot of veins of thought;

[]: Re: Evangelion and Marxism             Quote Post by pwhodges » Wed Oct 19, 2016 2:20 am Sachi wrote: He used the Eva for a selfish, narrow-minded reason,

[]: Re: Evangelion and Marxism             Quote Post by one-eyed » Wed Oct 19, 2016 10:59 am Sachi wrote: It doesn't matter whether or not he knew what would happen (he didn't), but he did verbally say something along the lines of "I don't care what happens to the world, I just want to save Ayanami". He used the Eva for a selfish, narrow-minded reason, and he betrayed the trust of those who were are counting on him. 3I was the unintended and unforeseen consequence of his selfish action, a metaphor for his betrayal of community.

[]: Re: Evangelion and Marxism             Quote Post by Lavinius » Thu Oct 20, 2016 9:23 am I neither know nor care much about Marxism, but it's clear that Eva has some relation to Fichte , who influenced Hegel who influenced Marx. As for Eva's political leanings- and Gunbuster's for that matter-, I don't think holding to a race's right to exterminate others and destroy the world for its own survival is very Marxi- oh wait it is.

Of course, this doesn't necessarily mean Marxism, but there is definitely a positive view of collectivism portrayed in Eva, as well as individualism.
